narrative: As an Asian male applicant, your law school application was evaluated using a model trained on data from thousands of students who took the bar exam, focusing on factors like academic records, test scores, and background to predict first-time pass rates. The model assessed your profile— including your undergraduate GPA of 2.50, LSAT score of 40, Year 3 school decile ranking of 3, middle family income bracket, and full-time student status—and predicted a 60% probability that you would fail the bar exam on your first attempt. This elevated risk of failure is why your application was rejected, as the school prioritizes admitting students likely to succeed on the bar to maintain their strong pass rates. Among all the details in your profile, three factors had the strongest sway in this decision: your LSAT score, undergraduate GPA, and Year 3 decile ranking. Your LSAT score of 40, which stands out above the typical 36.7 average, was the most influential positive factor, strongly pushing the prediction toward you passing the bar rather than failing. In contrast, your undergraduate GPA of 2.50, notably lower than the average of 3.21, pulled significantly in the opposite direction, increasing the model's concern about failure risk. Similarly, your Year 3 decile of 3, below the average of 5.53, added to that concern by signaling relatively weaker performance compared to peers in your school. Overall, while your LSAT showed real promise, the gaps in your GPA and later-year rankings tipped the scales toward a failure prediction when stacked against typical successful applicants, who often have stronger GPAs and higher decile placements. This doesn't reflect your full potential or personal drive, especially as a full-time student from a middle-income background where many others have succeeded. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to build on your already solid score, bolstering your GPA through post-baccalaureate courses or a master's program, and seeking targeted academic advising to improve your class rankings. Reapplying after addressing these areas could shift your profile into a much stronger position, as even small improvements in GPA and deciles often make a big difference for applicants like you. Many students in similar situations have turned things around with focused effort—your next steps can do the same.
